course_title: Indigenous Languages: Understanding Language Descriptions for Access and Use
course_description: Language materials and language descriptions are frequently locked away from community members and their language revitalisation efforts, in archives and in technical and reference grammars. This course critically reviews language description and collection and investigates Indigenous Cultural and Intellectual Protocols and documentation practices through the lens of Indigenising Linguistics. Students will gain detailed understanding and application of technical language description and analysis of sound patterns, phrase, word and clause structures, and cultural meanings embedded in language.

prerequisites: You'll need to complete the following courses before enrolling in this one:

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable

Additional timetable information
Please ensure your travel booking for the workshop 3-7 November is in place.
course_aims: This course aims to develop students' understanding of critical frameworks and best practice in language resource management and description. It aims to equip students with linguistic terminology and techniques to access and repurpose technical language descriptions.

learning_outcomes.description: Critically engage with and contribute to current discussions on language and collections management and access, Indigenous Cultural and Intellectual Property rights and the opportunities and challenges presented by digital technologies.
Apply linguistic frameworks and terminology to analyse Indigenous languages.

Identify and analyse cultural meanings embedded in language features, recognizing the inseparable connection between language, culture, and identity and apply this to enrich language description and revitalisation practices.
Repurpose and share specialist language knowledge with a range of audiences and for diverse purposes.

assessment_details.title: Case Study Report
assessment_details.task_description: In this assessment, you will select an archival or published language resource to critically evaluate and write a report that: introduces the context of your resource in general terms, e.g., situating it in terms of language group, location, people, personal experience and/or material discussed in class describes the key aspects of a language resource or an archival object including author/s, speaker/s, format, location held, archival status, etc. describes the intended purpose of the language resource or archival object critically evaluates strengths and weaknesses of the resource using criteria of discoverability, accessibility, use and reliability connects your evaluation to the readings and other class materials on the topic demonstrates independent research, critical reading, synthesis and structuring Expected length of the report is approximately 1200 words (no longer than 1500). Proposed structure and style of your report will be discussed in class. Statement on Generative AI & MT Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. assessment_details.title: Own Language Description and Resource Creation
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment involves creating a language resource. You must design it for a specific purpose, identifying a strategy to distribute it and reflecting on how it meets the intended purpose. The resource may be in print, digital, video or other format as negotiated with the lecturer. The resource must apply aspects of language description (e.g. sounds, spelling, word classes, suffixes, phrases, sentence structure, cultural meanings embedded in language, etc.). The assignment will include a copy of the resource itself and a commentary, which must include: An overview explaining the intended context, audience and purpose of the resource. A plan for a distribution and archiving strategy appropriate to the resource and your context. A critical reflection that evaluates the strengths and weaknesses of your resource in meeting its intended purpose. Commentary word length 1200 words =/- 10% Generative AI & MT Statement Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Language Learning Journal
assessment_details.due_date: 24/11/2025 5:00 pm Submit through Turnitin on Blackboard.
assessment_details.task_description: Task Description – Language learning is an on-going journey. This assessment is designed to encourage you to dedicate some time to your own language learning. For this task you should practice language weekly and write a weekly journal entry, for four weeks from 27th October to 22th November. In this course, you are learning about linguistic concepts and methods. This might inform the focus for your language learning. You may align your language learning to the language resource you create for task 3. The length guide per entry is 200 words (+/- 10%). Your entry should include: Include descriptions of: your language learning goals language materials you accessed an aspect of your language, using a combination of plain language and linguistic terminology language learning strategies you have tried Reflect on your above descriptions to include: new learnings about your language reflections on your engagement with people or resources helpful and unhelpful strategies or ideas ideas for future language learning pathways links between theory or readings and real-life situations Word limit for your journal is 200 words per entry, and a total of 800 words +/- %10. Statement on Generative AI & MT Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.topic: Pre-Workshop On-line Session 1 Guest talk by Dr Lesley Woods. Indigenous Linguistics. Accessible language description: Issues and solutions A proud Ngiyampaa Wangaaypuwan woman, Lesley holds a PhD in Linguistics from the Australian National University. She brings more than 20 years of experience working ethically and collaboratively with Aboriginal communities across New South Wales and Western Australia. Lesley’s work is grounded in a strong commitment to linguistic justice for First Nations peoples. Lesley is Regional Coordinator and Linguist at Muurrbay Aboriginal Language and Culture Co-operative. She oversees support for seven coastal Aboriginal languages and works closely with communities to provide expert linguistic guidance, develop and deliver language courses, and offer support to enhance language revitalisation efforts.

Intensive Day 1 Monday – Session 1 People and Languages in the room Getting going on linguistic description Decolonising Linguistics: Language materials and language descriptions – purposes, authors, audiences Exploring grammar in communication and language functions Monday – Session 2 Guest talk: Katie Jepson Phonology – Sounds and spelling Describing and comparing sounds across languages looking at sounds in combinations and in natural speech More language functions - phrases for everyday communication and what they tell us about the structure of language Monday – Session 3 Investigating ways that culture is embedded in language pronoun systems semantics, metaphorics and meaning extension Assignment planning: Own Language Description and Resource Creation (Task 3)

Intensive Day 2 Tuesday – Sessions 1 & 2 Describing Language with technical linguistic terminology Names, nouns and associated classes in Indigenous Languages Word-building, exploring morphology Tuesday – Session 3 Guest talk by Dr Rose Barrowcliffe on best practice in representation and access to archives and frameworks for Indigenous records, materials and collections, including Indigenous Cultural and Intellectual Property (ICIP) rights. Rose Barrowcliffe is a Butchulla woman, a post-doctoral research fellow at Macquarie University and a chief investigator for the Language Data Commons of Australia Australia. Rose’s research examines the representation of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people in archives and on embedding Indigenous perspectives in metadata to support discoverability and access of records for Indigenous peoples. Time on assignments - Case study of a language resource (Task 2)

Intensive Day 3 Wednesday – Session 1 & 2 Assessment task 2 - Problem set 1. Nouns and word building Verbs and associated classes in Indigenous Languages Tenses, aspects, and moods - three interrelated but distinct grammatical categories to map relationships between verb expressions verb classes - stems and endings Wednesday – Session 3 Activity – apply course insights into verbs in your or a related Language Time on Assignments - planning Own Language Resource (Task 3)

Intensive Day 4 Thursday – Sessions 1 & 2 Assessment task 1. Problem set 2 - Verbs Gari Tudor-Smith Sentence-building in Indigenous Languages (Syntax - part 1) Transitive vs intransitive in sentences Case marking for grammatical relations Relationships between words, phrases & verbs (valency) Word order Session 3 Syntax - sentence building (part 2) Question words: demonstratives, interrogatives Complex sentences Discussion: Enriching language description and revitalisation practices connections between language, culture, country and identity decolonising approaches to language description Time on assignments: Apply insights for own resource creation and reflection document (Task 3)

Intensive Day 5 Friday – Session 1 Assessment 1. Problem set 3. Sentences & Syntax Guest talk: Ben Foley - Digital technology for Language revitalisation A classification of digital technologies in language revitalisation Session 2 Language and the arts – reviving language in arts-based expression Proposed visit to UQ Art Museum Friday – Session 3 Wrap up on sentence-building in Indigenous Languages (part 2) Review of assessment tasks 2 & 3 progress
Post Workshop On-line Session 1 Review of Language description concepts. Assessment 2 & 3 check in.
Post Workshop On-line Session 2 Review and wrap up. Peer learning - sharing features of your language
